junior.title: Junior Data Engineer Terms
junior.description: Essential terms every aspiring data engineer should know

ETL.definition: Extract, Transform, Load - the process of extracting data from sources, transforming it to fit business needs, and loading it into a data warehouse
ETL.example: ETL pipeline that extracts customer data from MySQL, transforms it to remove duplicates, and loads it into Snowflake

Data Pipeline.definition: A series of data processing steps where output from one step becomes input for the next
Data Pipeline.example: A pipeline that reads CSV files → validates data → transforms to JSON → stores in database

Database.definition: An organized collection of structured information stored electronically
Database.example: PostgreSQL database storing customer orders and product information

SQL.definition: Structured Query Language - used to communicate with and manipulate databases

Schema.definition: The structure that defines how data is organized in a database
Schema.example: Table schema with columns: id (int), name (varchar), email (varchar)

API.definition: Application Programming Interface - a way for different software applications to communicate
API.example: REST API endpoint /api/users that returns customer data in JSON format

JSON.definition: JavaScript Object Notation - a lightweight data interchange format

CSV.definition: Comma-Separated Values - a simple file format for storing tabular data

Data Quality.definition: The measure of data accuracy, completeness, and reliability
Data Quality.example: Checking for null values, duplicate records, and valid email formats

CRUD.definition: Create, Read, Update, Delete - the four basic operations for managing data
CRUD.example: INSERT (Create), SELECT (Read), UPDATE, DELETE operations in SQL

mid.title: Mid-Level Data Engineer Terms
mid.description: Advanced concepts for growing data engineers

Data Warehouse.definition: A central repository that stores integrated data from multiple sources for analysis and reporting
Data Warehouse.example: Snowflake data warehouse consolidating sales data from Salesforce, web analytics, and ERP systems

Data Lake.definition: A storage repository that holds vast amounts of raw data in its native format
Data Lake.example: Azure Data Lake storing unstructured logs, images, videos, and structured data files

Apache Kafka.definition: A distributed streaming platform for building real-time data pipelines and streaming applications
Apache Kafka.example: Kafka streaming customer clicks from website to analytics systems in real-time

Apache Airflow.definition: An open-source platform to develop, schedule, and monitor workflows
Apache Airflow.example: Airflow DAG that runs daily ETL jobs with dependencies and error handling

Star Schema.definition: A database schema design with a central fact table connected to dimension tables
Star Schema.example: Sales fact table (center) connected to Customer, Product, and Time dimension tables

Dimensional Modeling.definition: A data structure technique optimized for data warehouse queries and reporting
Dimensional Modeling.example: Organizing data into facts (measurable events) and dimensions (descriptive attributes)

Data Mart.definition: A subset of a data warehouse focused on a specific business function or department
Data Mart.example: Marketing data mart containing only customer demographics and campaign performance data

OLTP vs OLAP.definition: Online Transaction Processing (operational) vs Online Analytical Processing (analytical)
OLTP vs OLAP.example: OLTP: Order processing system; OLAP: Sales reporting and analysis system

Batch Processing.definition: Processing large volumes of data in scheduled intervals
Batch Processing.example: Daily batch job processing yesterday's transactions for reporting

Stream Processing.definition: Real-time processing of data as it arrives
Stream Processing.example: Processing credit card transactions instantly to detect fraud

Data Lineage.definition: The path data takes from source to destination, including all transformations
Data Lineage.example: Tracking how customer data flows from CRM → ETL → Data Warehouse → Dashboard

SCD.definition: Slowly Changing Dimensions - methods to handle changes in dimension data over time
SCD.example: Type 2 SCD keeping history when a customer moves to a new address

senior.title: Senior Data Engineer Terms
senior.description: Expert-level concepts for senior data engineers

Data Mesh.definition: A decentralized approach to data architecture where domain teams own their data products
Data Mesh.example: Each business unit (Sales, Marketing, Finance) manages their own data pipelines and APIs

Lambda Architecture.definition: A data processing architecture combining batch and real-time processing layers
Lambda Architecture.example: Batch layer for historical analysis + Speed layer for real-time alerts + Serving layer for queries

Kappa Architecture.definition: A simplified architecture using only stream processing for both real-time and batch workloads
Kappa Architecture.example: Using Kafka Streams to process both real-time events and reprocess historical data

Change Data Capture (CDC).definition: The process of identifying and capturing changes made to data in a database
Change Data Capture (CDC).example: Debezium capturing every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE from PostgreSQL to sync with data lake

Data Vault.definition: A database modeling methodology designed for data warehouses in big data environments
Data Vault.example: Hub (business keys), Link (relationships), Satellite (descriptive attributes) tables

Microservices Architecture.definition: Architectural style structuring an application as a collection of loosely coupled services
Microservices Architecture.example: Separate services for data ingestion, transformation, quality checks, and serving

Event Sourcing.definition: Storing all changes to application state as a sequence of events
Event Sourcing.example: Bank account balance calculated from sequence of deposit/withdrawal events

CQRS.definition: Command Query Responsibility Segregation - separating read and write operations
CQRS.example: Write commands to OLTP database, read queries from optimized OLAP views

Polyglot Persistence.definition: Using different data storage technologies for different data types and use cases
Polyglot Persistence.example: PostgreSQL for transactions, MongoDB for documents, Redis for caching, Elasticsearch for search

Data Catalog.definition: A centralized inventory of data assets with metadata for discovery and governance
Data Catalog.example: Apache Atlas cataloging all datasets with schemas, owners, and data lineage

DataOps.definition: Collaborative data management practice focused on improving communication and automation
DataOps.example: CI/CD pipelines for data with automated testing, monitoring, and deployment

Data Governance.definition: Framework ensuring data is properly managed, secure, and compliant with regulations
Data Governance.example: GDPR compliance policies for customer data access, retention, and deletion

meta_infra.title: Meta Data Infrastructure Terms
meta_infra.description: Key technologies and concepts in Meta's data platform

Presto.definition: Distributed SQL query engine for interactive analytics on petabyte-scale data
Presto.example: SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM user_events WHERE ds = '2026-03-01' GROUP BY 1

Hive.definition: Data warehouse system built on Hadoop for reading, writing, and managing large datasets in HDFS
Hive.example: Partitioned Hive table storing daily snapshots of 2B user profiles by ds (datestamp)

Scribe.definition: Meta's distributed log transport system for real-time data ingestion at massive scale
Scribe.example: Scribe collecting 500B+ events/day from mobile apps, web, and backend services

Dataswarm.definition: Meta's internal workflow orchestrator (similar to Airflow) for scheduling and managing data pipelines
Dataswarm.example: Dataswarm DAG running hourly ETL: ingest → transform → load into warehouse tables

Scuba.definition: Real-time analytics system for interactive exploration of event data with sub-second latency
Scuba.example: Product team using Scuba to monitor News Feed engagement metrics in real-time

XStream.definition: Stream processing framework built on Apache Flink for real-time data transformations
XStream.example: XStream pipeline computing real-time ad impression counts for budget pacing

Data Catalog.definition: Centralized metadata system for discovering, understanding, and governing data assets across Meta
Data Catalog.example: Engineers searching the catalog to find the canonical user_dim table and its SLA

ds (Datestamp).definition: Standard partition column in Meta's data warehouse representing the date a snapshot was taken
ds (Datestamp).example: WHERE ds = '2026-03-01' — queries a single day's partition instead of scanning all data

Fact & Dimension Tables.definition: Core modeling pattern at Meta: facts store events/metrics, dimensions store entity attributes
Fact & Dimension Tables.example: fact_impressions joined with dim_users and dim_campaigns for ad analytics

Sessionization.definition: Grouping user events into sessions based on inactivity timeout (typically 30 min at Meta)
Sessionization.example: LAG(event_ts) to detect gaps > 30 min, then SUM(new_session_flag) for session IDs

Retention Analysis.definition: Measuring how many users return after first use, a key metric across all Meta products
Retention Analysis.example: D1/D7/D28 retention cohorts for Instagram Reels launch analysis

A/B Testing (Quick Experiments).definition: Meta's framework for running controlled experiments to measure feature impact on metrics
A/B Testing (Quick Experiments).example: Testing News Feed ranking change: 5% holdout group, measuring DAU and session time

PyTorch.definition: Meta's open-source deep learning framework used for ML model training and inference
PyTorch.example: Training content recommendation model on distributed GPU clusters

FBLearner.definition: Meta's ML platform for training, deploying, and managing machine learning models at scale
FBLearner.example: FBLearner pipeline: feature engineering → model training → A/B test → production rollout

tools.title: Essential Data Engineering Tools
tools.description: Common tools and platforms used in data engineering

Apache Spark.definition: Unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing with built-in modules for SQL, streaming, ML
Apache Spark.example: Processing 10TB of customer data across 100 nodes for monthly analytics reports
Apache Spark.when_to_use: Large-scale data processing, ETL jobs, machine learning pipelines

Databricks.definition: Unified platform combining Apache Spark with collaborative notebooks and MLOps capabilities
Databricks.example: Data scientists and engineers collaborating on customer churn prediction model
Databricks.when_to_use: Collaborative analytics, machine learning workflows, complex data transformations

Snowflake.definition: Cloud-based data warehouse platform with automatic scaling and separation of compute/storage
Snowflake.example: Enterprise data warehouse storing 50TB of structured data with instant scaling for reporting
Snowflake.when_to_use: Data warehousing, business intelligence, analytical workloads

dbt (data build tool).definition: Tool for transforming data in your warehouse using SQL and software engineering best practices
dbt (data build tool).example: Version-controlled SQL transformations with testing and documentation for customer metrics
dbt (data build tool).when_to_use: Data transformation, maintaining data models, analytics engineering

Apache Kafka.definition: Distributed streaming platform for building real-time data pipelines and applications
Apache Kafka.example: Streaming millions of IoT sensor readings per second for real-time monitoring
Apache Kafka.when_to_use: Real-time data streaming, event-driven architectures, microservices communication

Apache Airflow.definition: Platform to programmatically author, schedule and monitor workflows
Apache Airflow.example: Orchestrating daily ETL pipeline with 20 dependent tasks and error handling
Apache Airflow.when_to_use: Workflow orchestration, ETL scheduling, complex data pipeline management

Terraform.definition: Infrastructure as Code tool for building, changing, and versioning infrastructure
Terraform.example: Defining entire cloud data platform infrastructure in code for reproducible deployments
Terraform.when_to_use: Infrastructure automation, environment consistency, cloud resource management

Docker.definition: Platform for developing, shipping, and running applications in containers
Docker.example: Packaging data pipeline with all dependencies for consistent deployment across environments
Docker.when_to_use: Application packaging, environment consistency, microservices deployment

Kubernetes.definition: Container orchestration platform for automating deployment, scaling, and management
Kubernetes.example: Auto-scaling Spark jobs based on data volume and cluster resource availability
Kubernetes.when_to_use: Container orchestration, auto-scaling applications, cloud-native architectures

Apache Nifi.definition: Data integration tool for automating the flow of data between disparate systems
Apache Nifi.example: Visual data flow connecting 50 different data sources with transformation and routing
Apache Nifi.when_to_use: Data integration, real-time data flows, complex routing and transformation

Elasticsearch.definition: Distributed search and analytics engine built on Apache Lucene
Elasticsearch.example: Full-text search across millions of customer support tickets with sub-second response
Elasticsearch.when_to_use: Search functionality, log analytics, real-time data exploration

Redis.definition: In-memory data structure store used as database, cache, and message broker
Redis.example: Caching frequently accessed customer profiles to reduce database load by 80%
Redis.when_to_use: Caching, session storage, real-time analytics, pub/sub messaging

MinIO.definition: High-performance object storage compatible with Amazon S3 APIs
MinIO.example: On-premises object storage for data lake holding petabytes of unstructured data
MinIO.when_to_use: Object storage, data lake storage, S3-compatible storage on-premises

Apache Iceberg.definition: Open table format for huge analytic datasets with ACID transactions and schema evolution
Apache Iceberg.example: Maintaining consistent views of evolving customer data across multiple analytics tools
Apache Iceberg.when_to_use: Data lake table management, schema evolution, time travel queries

Great Expectations.definition: Python library for data validation, documentation, and profiling
Great Expectations.example: Automated data quality checks ensuring customer email formats are valid in every batch
Great Expectations.when_to_use: Data quality validation, pipeline testing, data documentation
